mechanism_description: Enable interconnection and deploy energy storage to improve grid flexibility and allow higher renewable energy penetration, reducing reliance on fossil generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid operations + generation dispatch → ↑ flexibility/renewables integration → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed human activity/system: Utilities/grid operators and markets deploy energy storage (batteries, pumped hydro, thermal storage) and improve interconnection/transmission to move and time-shift electricity, provide ancillary services, and relieve congestion.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Storage charges when low-carbon/low-cost electricity is available and discharges during peaks; it provides fast frequency response, reserves, and ramping support, reducing curtailment of renewables and reducing the need for fossil peaker/ramping units. Interconnection allows geographic smoothing and access to diverse generation resources.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower combustion of coal/oil/gas for electricity (and potentially avoided new fossil capacity), yielding net reductions in CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from the power system.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced power-sector GHG emissions by enabling more renewable generation and displacing fossil generation through improved grid flexibility.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Storage + interconnection → improved flexibility → higher renewable penetration and lower curtailment → reduced fossil dispatch/capacity → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While storage can also improve resilience/reliability (adaptation-adjacent) and improve energy productivity (resource efficiency), the principal climate mechanism described is enabling renewable integration and reducing fossil generation—direct emissions mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If storage is charged primarily with fossil generation or used mainly for backup/resilience without changing generation mix, mitigation benefits can be smaller or ambiguous; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly targets renewable integration and fossil displacement, so mitigation remains primary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—deploying storage/interconnection to increase renewable penetration—has its biggest climate-relevant effect through power-sector emissions reductions from fossil displacement.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy is primarily batteries vs transmission, utility-scale vs behind-the-meter, or includes explicit resilience goals requires policy-level evidence and is not set at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impacts depend on charging mix, marginal generator dynamics, and whether storage enables additional fossil generation cycling; some implementations may primarily serve reliability/resilience goals. Net mitigation is strongest when paired with high renewable penetration, clean charging constraints, or market designs that reduce curtailment and peaker use.
co_benefits: Potential grid reliability/resilience benefits during extreme heat and storms; potential air quality benefits from reduced peaker plant operation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Text is too brief to determine whether the intended instrument is infrastructure investment (e.g., new transmission) or a regulatory/market reform; classified as planning-strategy because it is framed as a “Strategy” without implementation details.
